title: Cleveland Browns Agree to Deal with Tight End Jack Stoll - 247 Sports
description:
According to Jeremy Fowler of ESPN, the Cleveland Browns have agreed to a one-year deal with free agent tight end Jack Stoll.
He would be the first move the Browns have made at tight end so far in free agency.
Stoll likely will not be the only move the Browns make at the tight end position.
More than likely, Stoll projects to compete for a third tight end spot who contributes on special teams.
The Browns will still be looking to add more talent to the offensive line as well as the tight end room over the course of the offseason.
text:
According to Jeremy Fowler of ESPN, the Cleveland Browns have agreed to a one-year deal with free agent tight end Jack Stoll. The financial terms have not been disclosed.

Stoll played collegiately at Nebraska, signing as an undrafted free agent with the Philadelphia Eagles. He appeared in 68 games with the Eagles from 2021 to 2024, credited with 28 starts over that period. He split time between the Eagles and the Miami Dolphins in 2024 and then appeared in 15 games as a member of the New Orleans Saints in 2025.

He has 28 career receptions for 239 yards and one touchdown. His most productive season was 2022, when he caught 11 passes for 123 yards.

Stoll's deal cannot be officially signed until the start of the league year, which begins at 4pm. He would be the first move the Browns have made at tight end so far in free agency. The Browns are set to lose long-time tight end David Njoku to free agency. They have Harold Fannin, who was outstanding in his rookie season. However, he is a space tight end. Njoku played inline. That's the hole the Browns need to fill over, trying to improve their effectively on the line of scrimmage. According to PFF, 69 percent of Stoll's snaps have been inline over the course of his career.

Stoll likely will not be the only move the Browns make at the tight end position. They currently need bodies. More than likely, Stoll projects to compete for a third tight end spot who contributes on special teams. That is where he's gotten the bulk of his reps over the course of his career. In all, he has 928 snaps on special teams ranging from kick return, punt coverage and field goal.

To this point, the Browns have spent a significant amount of time addressing the offensive line of scrimmage. They have made four moves on the offensive line including re-signing Teven Jenkins, agreeing to contracts with Elgton Jenkins and Zion Johnson and a trade to acquire Teven Jenkins.

Thus far, the Browns have made six moves, five of which have come on the offensive side of the ball and all five have been in an effort to improve the talent on the line of scrimmage. With the amount of turnover coming in the form of free agent departures and the potential retirement of Joel Bitonio, the Browns have focused their attention on those areas of the roster. The offensive line struggled last season due to injuries and inconsistency, which short-circuited so much of what the Browns hoped to achieve on the offensive side of the ball.

The Browns will still be looking to add more talent to the offensive line as well as the tight end room over the course of the offseason.

title: Callahan: NFL rivals generally praise Patriots' free agency - Boston Herald

text:
The league approves.

The Patriots’ free-agent class drew consistent praise from several sources around the NFL who were reached by the Herald at the end of Day 3.

“I like them all on the surface. Good values,” an AFC front-office executive told me. “(Alijah) Vera-Tucker is obviously the risk, injury-wise.”

Vera-Tucker, an uber-talented offensive guard, represented a turning point for the Patriots on Monday night, when he headlined a three-player group that now includes up to six new starters. Most of them are clear upgrades from veterans the Patriots have parted with via release or trade.

There’s Vera-Tucker, who will fill the left guard spot Jared Wilson vacated to succeed Garrett Bradbury at center. He’s the Patriots’ new best offensive lineman.

On Wednesday, safety Kevin Byard signed. He’s a reigning All-Pro whose seven interceptions in Chicago last year almost matched Jaylinn Hawkins’ career interception total (eight). Any questions?

Ex-Bills bulldozer Reggie Gilliam is a real fullback replacing a converted one. No argument there.

And Romeo Doubs, an ascending 26-year-old replacing Stefon Diggs, 32, as Drake Maye’s best receiver? Any upgrade there is a projection. Though, not according to one NFC assistant coach.

“(It’s an) upgrade because you need to feature Diggs,” the coach texted. “You’ve gotta make sure Diggs is getting his touches otherwise he is a pain. Now they have a good, selfless guy who will block. Smart pickup.”

As of late Wednesday night, Doubs received the largest free-agent contract the Patriots agreed to at four years, $68 million. The Pats reached terms after the best available wideout, Alec Pierce, came off the board at $116 million early Monday. The Patriots ultimately rebounded from losing the Pierce sweepstakes by settling for the market’s No. 2 receiver, who had 279 fewer yards last season and took $48 million less.

Those decisions spoke to the value multiple executives referenced when analyzing the Pats’ free-agent hail. One longtime pro personnel executive, however, disagreed, citing Vera Tucker’s contract (three years, $42 million) and the deal the Patriots gave outside linebacker Dre’Mont Jones (three years, $36.5 million).

“Seems a little high for Jones, but he’s a good rusher who was solid for Baltimore down the stretch,” he said. “I thought that was a little high for (Vera-Tucker) coming off injury and the durability issues throughout his career.”

Vera-Tucker’s contract drew the most scrutiny among league sources. By now, you’ve heard he played just 43 out of a possible 84 games in New York, where he ranked among the NFL’s best offensive lineman in his last full season of 2024. But before and after that year he was sidelined for good with separate torn triceps injuries and a ruptured Achilles.

The Patriots, given that history, baked $12.5 million worth of per-game roster bonuses into his contract to protect against injury. But the total money and guaranteed dollars ($21 million) spoke loudest: they believe he’ll stay healthy enough.

“Damn good player when he’s available,” the AFC executive wrote.

Oddly enough, the free-agent addition who received universal praise was Gilliam. The 28-year-old fullback is expected to unlock chapters of the Patriots’ playbook that went uncalled last season. Or, when used, were ineffective.

“It looks like they expect fullback to play a bigger role this year. And he’s a really good special teams player,” another AFC executive wrote. “The contract kind of reflects that.”

The NFC coach agreed.

“Gilliam is a great signing because (Patriots offensive coordinator Josh McDaniels) needs a fullback in his system,” he said. “Westover isn’t it.”

Byard’s deal also drew praise. The pro personnel evaluator believes the 32-year-old still has a few good seasons left, and if he doesn’t, the Patriots protected themselves with a one-year contract.

None of the league sources offered thoughts on the signings of backup tight end Julian Hill or safety Mike Brown. The Patriots may not need them anyway.

By Day 3, even with outstanding needs at tight end and edge rusher, most of their free-agent work is done.

text:
FRISCO, Texas — For the second and third time in a three-day span, the Dallas Cowboys have struck a deal to trade with another team but, this time, they're sending players out of North Texas, namely, defensive tackles Osa Odighizuwa and Solomon Thomas.

The Cowboys have agreed to terms with the San Francisco 49ers to send Odighizuwa to the Bay Area in exchange for a third-round pick (92nd overall) in the 2026 NFL Draft, and Thomas is heading to the Tennessee Titans for a seventh-round swap in April. The Cowboys now pick No. 218 in exchange for No. 225.

The deals come a few months after Dallas traded to acquire both Pro Bowler Kenny Clark and All-Pro Quinnen Williams, revamping the defensive tackle position in a major way, recently signing Otito Ogbonnia to join second-year player Jay Toia as depth, having recently waived Perrion Winfrey.

Odighizuwa, a former third-round pick of the Cowboys in 2021, he was selected out of UCLA as a former First-Team All-Pac 12 talent that, eventually, steadily increased his production at the NFL level and his ability to disrupt games with each passing season.

Despite having to adjust to a one-year scheme under Mike Zimmer, he was still able to notch a career-high in sacks (4.5), tackles (47) and quarterback hits (23 total — 10 more than his second-best season in that category) that season.

This led to the Cowboys awarding him a four-year, $80 million contract extension in March 2025.

His production stalled a bit in 2025, however, as he worked to try and carve a groove out of Matt Eberflus' system, a tall task, particularly following the additions of Clark and Williams. He will now get a fresh start with the 49ers.

Thomas joined the team last year on a two-year deal, and though he battled injury during the 2025 season, he proved himself a worthy rotational talent, joining Robert Saleh's group in Nashville — also reuniting with former Cowboys' defensive line coach Aaron Whitecotton (and for the third time in his career), now serving as defensive run game coordinator and defensive line coach for Saleh and the Titans.

The Cowboys will move forward now having a third-round pick, having not had one prior, and $16 million in salary that will be accountable to San Francisco, with Dallas landing $4.75 million in overall cap savings from Odighizuwa's trade and another $2.5 million by moving on from Thomas.

title: Packers injury recovery timelines: Micah Parsons, Tucker Kraft and more
description:
For the ACL tears, I want to work with the Christian Watson timeline, since it’s most recent example in the minds of Packers fans.
This is fairly common, as clean ACL tears are considered to have around nine-ish-month recovery timelines for NFL players.
Micah Parsons, OLB We’re going to work with the same Watson timeline assumptions here for Micah Parsons.
Nine months from that day, the Watson return to practice timeline, will be mid-September.
Tucker Kraft and Zach Tom should start practicing with the Packers in August and be ready to go by Week 1.
text:
I was originally going to get up a 53-man roster projection for Thursday morning (stay tuned for that tomorrow), but while doing the exercise, I realized that I was doing a lot of work trying to figure out when guys were expected to return from injury after the chaos that happened at the end of last season. So instead of a Green Bay Packers roster projection, let’s talk about the team’s notable injuries going into 2026 and the timelines that we can expect from these players.

Here is my disclaimer that I am in no way a doctor, nor have I ever had any experience in injury recovery, other than tearing my knee up twice. Here is all of my “expert” knowledge: Tearing your knee up sucks; Don’t do it. I will be leaning on the knowledge of others.

Tucker Kraft, TE

We’re going to have to make some assumptions here. For the ACL tears, I want to work with the Christian Watson timeline, since it’s most recent example in the minds of Packers fans. As far as we know, the ACL injuries that Green Bay’s players have suffered are clean ACL tears.

Watson tore his ACL on January 5th, 2025. He returned to action on October 26th, 2025. This is fairly common, as clean ACL tears are considered to have around nine-ish-month recovery timelines for NFL players. Thank God for modern medicine. Watson’s return to game action took 294 days, about 9 months and 21 days.

Tucker Kraft tore his ACL on November 2nd, 2025. 294 days from that date is August 23rd, 2026, so he should be game-ready for the Packers at some point during training camp. For what it’s worth, Watson first practiced with Green Bay on October 6th, almost 9 months to the day from his ACL tear, so we can expect Kraft to return to the practice field some time in early August, even if he’s not working full-speed by then.

Micah Parsons, OLB

We’re going to work with the same Watson timeline assumptions here for Micah Parsons. Parsons tore his ACL on December 14th, 2025. Nine months from that day, the Watson return to practice timeline, will be mid-September.

The obstacle that Parsons will face is that mid-September is after the cutdown deadline, meaning that he’ll still be on the physically unable to perform list, but that players who start on the physically able to perform list in the regular season must miss the first four games of the season.

On paper, he should be good to go by Game 5 of the the Packers’ season. The issue is that he won’t have the same two-week ramp up of practice (when he’ll still be locked on the PUP list) before a game week like Watson had. Because of the mechanics of the PUP list, there’s a good chance that Parsons will be ready to start practicing with the team before he’s actually allowed to practice with the team, due to league rules. I’m sure Green Bay has some sort of plan for him in this regard, where they can simulate a practice with him individually, and that this won’t be coming to them as a surprise in September.

Zach Tom, RT

Zach Tom stated to the press after the season that he had a partially torn patellar tendon, and that after his surgery there will be a six-month recovery process. As long as he had the surgery by mid-February, he should also be able to be good to go for the regular season (I’m giving him some time on this injury timeline for a Watson-like ramp-up period before the regular season).

Devonte Wyatt, DE

Devonte Wyatt’s broken fibula and torn ankle ligaments will take three-to-five months to recover from, according to his post-season media availability. Based on that timeline, he should also be good to go by the regular season, and there’s a chance that he’s ready to go by minicamp in June. Minicamp should be happening right around the later end of his estimation.

Jordon Riley, DE

Jordon Riley tore his Achilles tendon, a pretty brutal injury. Generally, these injuries take at least nine months to recover from and could take up to a year. He had his injury on December 27th, 2025, so at minimum he shouldn’t be counted on to return to action until Game 5 (because of the PUP rules and the quickest end of the injury timeline projection) and might not play in 2026 at all, depending on the situation.

Kamal Hadden, CB

Tom Silverstein of the Milwaukee Journal Sentinel reported that Kamal Hadden said after the season that the cornerback was told his ankle injury will take four to five months to recover from. On that timeline, Hadden could be ready to go by minicamp, too.

—

This honestly wasn’t as bad of an outlook as I thought it would be. Devonte Wyatt and Kamal Hadden are in play to return around minicamp in mid-June. I wouldn’t be surprised if the team was a little conservative and waited a bit later for them to start ramping up, since games still won’t be played for several months.

Tucker Kraft and Zach Tom should start practicing with the Packers in August and be ready to go by Week 1. Micah Parsons, unless he’s some sort of medical marvel, will probably be ready by Game 5 of the season, depending on how much practice time the team believes that he needs before he’s able to contribute in games.

Along with Parsons, defensive lineman Jordon Riley will also probably start the season on the PUP list, too. At absolute best, Riley will return around the same time as Parsons, but I just would count on him hanging on the injury list until we’re told otherwise. When it’s Riley time, they’ll let us know he’s getting closer to returning to the field.

text:
The Steelers signed free agent linebacker Cole Holcomb to a two-year contract.

Holcomb originally signed with the Steelers in 2023 as an unrestricted free agent and has had a rough road injury-wise early in his Steelers career.

He started the first eight games in 2023, recording 54 tackles, including 31 solo stops, four tackles for a loss, and two passes defensed, before suffering a devastating season-ending knee injury.

The injury forced Holcomb to miss the entire 2024 season as he went through rehab.

He beat the odds, returning in 2025 and appeared in 14 games, starting three. He had 28 tackles, 18 of them solo stops, a forced fumble and nine special teams tackles.

Holcomb was honored in 2025 at the Steelers Ed Block Courage Award recipient which was voted on by his teammates, and is given to a player who has shown courage either coming back from an injury or a life-altering situation.

For his career, Holcomb has appeared in 72 games, starting 59. He has 464 tackles, 282 solo stops, and 19 tackles for a loss. He has added four and a half sacks, 12 passes defensed, three interceptions, eight forced fumbles and two fumble recoveries.

Holcomb spent four seasons with the Washington Commanders (2019-2022) after they drafted him in the fifth round of the 2019 NFL Draft.

He played in 50 games in Washington, starting 48 of them. He recorded 382 tackles, including 233 solo stops, and 15 tackles for a loss. He had four and a half sacks, three interceptions, including one for a touchdown, and 10 passes defensed. He also had nine quarterback hits, five forced fumbles and one fumble recovery.

Holcomb, who was a defensive captain for the Commanders his final season there, had his 2022 season cut short when he suffered a foot injury that required season ending surgery. Before suffering the injury, he played in seven games, recording 69 tackles, 37 of them solo stops, and one pass defensed.

He has his best season in 2021, starting all 16 games and finishing with 142 tackles, 83 of them solo stops, and two interceptions.

Holcomb played collegiately at North Carolina, 2015-18, where he was a former walk-on. Holcomb had 328 tackles in college and was a second-team All-ACC selection his senior year.

title: Where is Tyreek Hill Going? Latest NFL Free Agency News - Heavy Sports
description:
Where is Tyreek Hill going?
How Serious Is Tyreek Hill’s Injury This is the biggest variable in the entire story.
That makes his market less about name value and more about recovery timeline, medical evaluations and whether teams believe he can still be Tyreek Hill on the other side of rehab.
Tyreek Hill Contract Hill’s contract is a huge part of why he is on the market.
Until teams get comfortable with Hill’s rehab timeline, “Where is Tyreek Hill going?” is more about possibilities than a real destination.
text:
Where is Tyreek Hill going? The clean answer right now is that no team has signed him yet, but he is available after the Dolphins released him on Feb. 16. That move was driven by both money and uncertainty: Spotrac says Miami created $22.8 million in cap space with the release, while NFL.com reported Hill is rehabbing a major leg injury and that it is unclear when, or even if, he will play in 2026.

That is the real “why today” hook. This is not a standard free-agent story built around a healthy star hitting the market at the perfect time. It is a wait-and-see story involving an elite receiver, a major injury, and a contract situation that made Miami’s decision easier once his 2026 money was about to become more expensive. ESPN reported Hill carried a $51.1 million cap hit for 2026 with no guaranteed money left, while Spotrac noted the Dolphins were staring at a March guarantee trigger before cutting him.

How Serious Is Tyreek Hill’s Injury

This is the biggest variable in the entire story. NFL.com reported Hill is rehabbing a major leg injury, and CBS Sports said he suffered a knee dislocation in Week 4, then underwent surgery for an ACL tear. That makes his market less about name value and more about recovery timeline, medical evaluations and whether teams believe he can still be Tyreek Hill on the other side of rehab.

There is also an older wrist issue in the background. NFL.com reported in November 2024 that Hill likely needed wrist surgery, but the current free-agency conversation is being driven much more by the leg injury and the uncertainty around his 2026 availability.

Tyreek Hill Contract

Hill’s contract is a huge part of why he is on the market. Spotrac says he signed a three-year, $90 million reworked extension with Miami in August 2024, but the Dolphins released him before more 2026 money kicked in. Spotrac’s Dolphins cap page shows Hill now counts $28.25 million in dead money for Miami in 2026.

The value add here is simple: this was not just a football call. It was a cap call, too. Spotrac’s Dolphins financial breakdown said $16 million of Hill’s 2026 salary was set to become fully guaranteed in March, making an early release widely expected.

Tyreek Hill Stats

Hill’s 2025 production was modest before the injury. ESPN lists him at 21 catches, 265 yards and 1 touchdown. For a player with Hill’s résumé, those numbers are not the selling point. The selling point is the larger body of work and whether teams believe his speed and explosion will return after rehab. For his career, Hill has 819 catches for 11,363 yards and 83 touchdowns.

He is also 32 years old, which matters because age plus a major knee injury is a harder bet than age alone. That combination is why Hill’s next destination may take longer to materialize than a typical star receiver move.

What Happens Next?

The next step is medical clarity. Until teams get comfortable with Hill’s rehab timeline, “Where is Tyreek Hill going?” is more about possibilities than a real destination. What is clear now is that he is available, unsigned, and one of free agency’s most complicated boom-or-bust cases.

authors: The Next Gen Stats Analytics Team

text:
CONTRACT: Three years, maximum value of $45 million.

Walker's decision to join the Chiefs projects as a spectacular move for both player and team. The MVP of Super Bowl LX will benefit from Kansas City's upper-echelon blocking; in 2025, the Chiefs' offensive line propelled the team's rushers to a 43.6% success rate (seventh-highest in the NFL). And K.C. achieved that despite being in desperate need of more dynamism from its running back room. Chiefs RBs have produced explosive runs of 10-plus yards at the lowest rate in the league in consecutive seasons (4.7% in 2024, 6.5% in 2025). Walker, on the other hand, generated explosive runs on 14.9% of his carries this past season, the second-highest rate among running backs with 150-plus attempts. His top career speed of 22.09 MPH is faster than any mark hit by a Chiefs back on a scrimmage play since at least 2016.

CONTRACT: Four years, $120 million.

The Panthers gave Phillips the largest contract a free-agent edge rusher has ever received, but it will be worth it if he can revitalize their beleaguered pass rush. Carolina's edges have recorded an average pressure rate below 10% in each of Ejiro Evero's three seasons coordinating the defense, producing a league-low mark of 8.7% in 2025. Evero has historically relied on a talent advantage to drive his best pass rushes, employing the likes of Aaron Donald, Leonard Floyd , Bradley Chubb and Brian Burns in the past. But he's had no players of that caliber at his disposal over the last two seasons. Phillips' 63 pressures in 2025 were more than twice the total of any Panthers player, and his 34 pressures with the Eagles led the team following their Week 9 bye. Phillips can also help Evero mix up his looks, having produced an otherworldly 33.3% pressure rate when rushing from the interior in 2025.

CONTRACT: Three years, $81 million.

Klint Kubiak's scheme is deeply reliant on interior protection, but the Raiders struggled at the center position as much as any team in 2025. To help their new coach, they landed the best center on the market by far. In 2025, Las Vegas centers allowed 9.5 sacks, the most in the NFL. Their blocking did not do much for Ashton Jeanty , either, as the rookie running back gained just 3.3 yards per carry on inside runs, the fewest of any player with at least 100 such attempts. Linderbaum has allowed 10 sacks in his entire four-year career while giving up a quick pressure on just 0.6% of pass blocks in 2025, the fourth-lowest mark at the position. In addition to anchoring the blocking for presumptive No. 1 overall pick Fernando Mendoza, the star center could help unlock Jeanty. During Linderbaum's time in Baltimore, Ravens RBs averaged 4.6 yards per inside run, the fourth-best mark in the NFL during that span.

CONTRACT: Three years, value TBA.

The six-time Pro Bowler joins a WR room that was depleted by injuries, one that returns fewer than 900 receiving yards from last season. Assuming Evans can bounce back from the most injury-marred season of his storied career, he should be a perfect "X" receiver for San Francisco. Brock Purdy and Mac Jones combined for five touchdowns and 11 interceptions when targeting receivers aligned wide last season, compared to 28 touchdowns and five interceptions targeting tight, slot or backfield alignments. Evans has aligned out wide on 76.1% of his snaps in the last decade and totaled at least five touchdowns from the alignment on his own in every season from 2018-2024. He has especially shined as the isolated receiver; in the last three seasons, his 13 touchdowns in such situations are tied with Davante Adams for the most in the NFL, while nobody else has reached double digits. Meanwhile, Evans has gained 2.3 yards per route run when aligned out wide since 2023, providing a stellar upgrade to a 49ers group that has produced an average of 1.6 in that time frame.

CONTRACT: Three years, $40.25 million.

Cook steps in for Geno Stone as the Bengals’ deep-middle safety, and the two have employed very similar strategies with different levels of effectiveness in recent years. Both played at an average depth of exactly 10.4 yards off the line of scrimmage in 2025, shallower than most safeties. Cook, however, is a more reliable deep man than anyone Cincinnati has employed since the days of Jessie Bates III and Vonn Bell. He has missed just 5.9% of his tackles over the past two seasons, the third-lowest rate among safeties with at least 100 opportunities, while Stone has missed tackles almost three times as often (15.9%) over that span. Cook is at his best as a single-high defender, having allowed three career receptions on 435 snaps in a single-high zone. He’ll be deployed in that role much more often with the Bengals, who played single-high coverage on 57.5% of snaps in 2025, than he was in Kansas City (45.2% in '25).

CONTRACT: Three years, $30 million.

Dennis Allen’s defense is based out of Cover 2, which relies on highly athletic linebackers to account for significant space in the middle of the field. After releasing Tremaine Edmunds, the Bears were left with just two off-ball LBs on the roster who played 50+ snaps last season, and neither is a natural Cover 2 anchor. Enter Bush, who allowed 0.49 yards per coverage snap last season, the second-fewest of any linebacker who logged at least 250 coverage snaps. He also recorded three interceptions (returning two for touchdowns) while allowing just one touchdown. He and Edmunds were two of the 14 LBs who were trusted to play man-to-man over 20% of the time, suggesting Allen will continue to cater to Bush's strengths. Bush was a lynchpin of the Browns’ run defense in 2025, as well, recording 75 run tackles, more than any Bears defender not named Roquan Smith in a single season over the last decade.

CONTRACT: One year, maximum value of $15 million.

Woolen was quietly an integral cog in Seattle's championship-winning defense last season, and he was at his best in man coverage. On 175 snaps in man, he allowed just 10 receptions for 85 yards on 32 targets, yielding the fewest yards per snap (0.49, minimum 100 snaps) and lowest completion percentage (31.3%, minimum 20 targets) among all cornerbacks. Eagles DC Vic Fangio used to avoid man coverage, but he gave his outside cornerbacks the fourth-most man assignments in the NFL last season (506). Woolen replaces Adoree' Jackson , who allowed a 53.8% completion rate and 1.43 yards per snap in man. The addition of Woolen also entrenches Eagles stars Quinyon Mitchell and Cooper DeJean at their best possible positions (boundary and slot, respectively), as Woolen will presumably serve as the full-time field-side corner, just as he did in Seattle.

CONTRACT: Two years, $17 million.

This former Florida Gator should immediately step in as the best coverage linebacker of Todd Bowles' tenure in Tampa. Over the past five seasons in Detroit, Anzalone allowed the second-lowest completion percentage in coverage of any LB (67.5%, minimum 100 targets) while recording 33 passes defensed, the fourth-most at the position. In 2025, Buccaneers linebackers allowed the highest completion percentage of any team's 'backer unit (84.4%) and recorded just eight passes defensed combined; Anzalone totaled nine on his own. Anzalone will also slide seamlessly into Bowles' blitz scheme. His 2025 numbers (17 pressures and 2.5 sacks on 91 pass rushes) almost perfectly match those of incumbent starter SirVocea Dennis (19 pressures and three sacks on 95 pass rushes).

Teel has a Grade 2 hamstring strain and will soon rejoin the Chicago White Sox spring training camp, Chris Getz told reporters, including Scott Merkin of MLB.com.

According to Rob Bradford of WEEI, Teel’s WBC roster spot could go to Boston Red Sox catcher Gasper. Bradford reported earlier Wednesday that Gasper was on his way to Houston to join Italy in light of Teel’s injury.

Teel left Italy’s game against the United States after he injured himself after sliding into second base in the sixth inning of a pool play game at Daikin Park. Italy beat the U.S., 8-6, in a surprising upset win.

Advertisement

Italy plays Mexico on March 11 and can advance to the quarterfinals of the tournament with a victory.

Teel’s absence could hurt Team Italy in the WBC. He went 2-for-2 before suffering the injury and was replaced by J.J. D’Orazio for the remainder of the game.

Advertisement

The White Sox also had Teel penciled in to potentially take the lion’s share of the playing time behind the plate in 2026. As a rookie last year, Teel slashed .273/.375/.411 in 78 games.

Now, Edgar Quero is poised to be the White Sox’s primary catcher to start the regular season. According to Merkin, Teel could be out for 4 to 6 weeks.

title: In surprise move, Giants re-sign OL Evan Neal, struggling 2022 top-10 pick - The Athletic
description:
But the New York Giants re-signing offensive lineman Evan Neal might be the biggest surprise.
The Giants are re-signing Neal to a one-year contract worth $1.2 million, the league minimum salary.
If ever a player appeared to need a change of scenery, it was Neal, the No.
7 pick in the 2022 NFL Draft who has been an epic bust during his first four seasons in New York.
Instead, Neal is back, trying to salvage his career in the same place it went off track.
text:
The Baltimore Ravens’ reversal on the Maxx Crosby trade with the Las Vegas Raiders is the biggest news of free agency. But the New York Giants re-signing offensive lineman Evan Neal might be the biggest surprise.

The Giants are re-signing Neal to a one-year contract worth $1.2 million, the league minimum salary. If ever a player appeared to need a change of scenery, it was Neal, the No. 7 pick in the 2022 NFL Draft who has been an epic bust during his first four seasons in New York.

Neal allowed eight sacks as the starting right tackle as a rookie. He struggled to stay healthy, missing 22 games in his first three seasons due to injury. In 2023, he infamously fired back at critics by blasting the team’s fans.

Neal got a clean slate when he switched from right tackle to guard last offseason. But he never seriously pushed veteran Greg Van Roten for the starting right guard job during training camp, then didn’t play a single snap all season. Neal was a healthy scratch for the first 10 games before landing on injured reserve with a hamstring injury in Week 11. He returned to practice in Week 16 and was promptly sidelined by a neck injury for the rest of the season.

Yet, Neal is back with the Giants. Clearly, new coach John Harbaugh had a favorable assessment of Neal from the 2022 draft process when the Alabama lineman was viewed as a top prospect. Harbaugh is showing a lot of faith in coaching being able to salvage Neal after two different offensive line coaches failed to fix the same balance and footwork issues that have plagued the 6-foot-7, 340-pounder throughout his career.

There’s no risk in bringing back the 25-year-old considering the contract, but it’s a bit concerning that the Giants don’t appear to have a starting right guard on the current roster. They surely will add someone — re-signing Van Roten remains a distinct possibility — but Neal can’t be a serious part of the plan.

If Neal comes in and blows everyone away with his improvement under a new coaching staff, it will make for an amazing story. But nothing from his first four seasons suggests that will happen. That’s why the expectation was that both sides would prefer a fresh start. Instead, Neal is back, trying to salvage his career in the same place it went off track.

title: Daniel Jones Announces Massive Injury Update After Colts Hand Him Historic NFL Contract
description:
Indianapolis Colts quarterback Daniel Jones has a message for next year.
With the focus shifting to the 2026 season, Jones spoke about how his recovery is continuing.
“My goal is to be back by week one and be ready to go,” Daniel Jones said on the Pat McAfee Show.
However, before Indianapolis, Jones had an ugly ending with the New York Giants, who drafted him sixth overall in 2019.
With this deal, Daniel Jones will make $88 million, which includes over $60 million in guaranteed money, as the two-year contract can go up to $100 million with incentives.
text:
Indianapolis Colts quarterback Daniel Jones has a message for next year. In 2025, Jones was on track for a career season, leading the team to a stunning 7-1 start before an injury ended his year. With the focus shifting to the 2026 season, Jones spoke about how his recovery is continuing.

“My goal is to be back by week one and be ready to go,” Daniel Jones said on the Pat McAfee Show. “I think that’s what we’re working towards and should be able to accomplish. So it’s all a process, and we’ll keep checking the boxes, working with the guys here with the Colts, and feel good about that. So I’m. I’m planning on being back and the goals shooting for week one.”

When asked about an update on his recovery process involving his Achilles issue, Jones stated his update clearly that his team “should be able to accomplish” being back by Week 1, to finish what he started: a quest for playoff football.

Advertisement

Ahead of the 2025 season, Daniel Jones joined the Colts on a one-year, $14 million deal in free agency last year and quickly revived his career. However, before Indianapolis, Jones had an ugly ending with the New York Giants, who drafted him sixth overall in 2019. He started 69 games for the Big Blue, posting a 24-44-1 record before his run with the organization came to an end as the Giants released him in November 2024 to conclude a tumultuous tenure in the Big Apple.

He signed with the Minnesota Vikings’ practice squad for the remainder of the season before joining the Colts.

Then, as Indianapolis’ QB1, Jones had thrown for 3,101 yards with 19 touchdowns and was just 104 yards short of his career-high for passing yards and six touchdowns away from his personal best in passing touchdowns.

Based on these stellar numbers before his injury, the Colts placed the rarely used transition tag on Jones before the March 3 deadline, which allowed the franchise to match any offer sheet he might have signed.

Advertisement

With this provision, the Indianapolis front office put forth a contract for the young quarterback that will be etched in league history.

“Max value of $100 million, making it the largest two-year contract in NFL history, per agents Brian Murphy and Andrew Kessler,” as reported by ESPN’s Adam Schefter. With this deal, Daniel Jones will make $88 million, which includes over $60 million in guaranteed money, as the two-year contract can go up to $100 million with incentives.

While the Indianapolis Colts have handed Daniel Jones a historic extension, this wasn’t the only big-money move made by the franchise this offseason, as they re-signed wide receiver Alec Pierce to a four-year, $116 million contract. When asked about this move, Jones shared his reaction on the Pat McAfee Show.

Daniel Jones expresses excitement about Pierce’s extension

Alongside Jones, the Colts were facing a significant decision as wide receiver Alec Pierce expressed a desire to test free agency. In the 2025 season, Pierce recorded 47 receptions for 1,003 receiving yards and 6 touchdowns while leading the NFL with a 21.3 yards-per-catch average for the second consecutive season.

Advertisement

With these stellar numbers, Pierce was retained by the Colts after handing him a four-year, $114 million deal. Reacting to this move, Jones shared he’s “fired up” that Pierce is back with the Colts on the Pat McAfee Show.

“He was a tremendous leader, obviously a great player, extremely productive and a guy you could count on,” Jones said.

With Daniel Jones targeting a Week 1 return and Alec Pierce locked in on a massive deal, the Indianapolis Colts appear poised to pick up right where they left off. The pieces are in place for a legitimate Super Bowl run in 2026.

title: Vic Fangio on Jihaad Campbell shoulder injury, offseason timeline - NBC10 Philadelphia
description:
Eagles linebacker Jihaad Campbell, a full-time starter now that Nakobe Dean is gone, is dealing with a serious shoulder injury that will sideline him for the next several months, defensive coordinator Vic Fangio said Wednesday.
“Well, he's got to be ready,” Fangio said.
“You know, he is dealing with a shoulder injury, he's gonna miss probably most of the offseason.
But he did appear on the injury report late in the season with what was called a “back/shoulder” injury.
With Dean gone and Campbell sidelined, the Eagles have Jeremiah Trotter Jr. and Smael Mondon as linebacker options until Campbell returns.
text:
Eagles linebacker Jihaad Campbell, a full-time starter now that Nakobe Dean is gone, is dealing with a serious shoulder injury that will sideline him for the next several months, defensive coordinator Vic Fangio said Wednesday.

NBC Sports Philadelphia’s John Clark caught up with Fangio at a reception for Hall of Fame coach Dick Vermeil at the Mission Capitalist Club in Whitemarsh Township, Montgomery County.

With Dean signing a three-year contract with the Raiders, Campbell takes over Dean’s starting linebacker spot.

“Well, he's got to be ready,” Fangio said. “And we’ve got to do a good job getting him ready.

“You know, he is dealing with a shoulder injury, he's gonna miss probably most of the offseason. So we're gonna have to cram it into him in training camp. And hopefully we have enough work to get that done for him.”

Campbell has been dealing with shoulder issues since his final year at Alabama. He said he got hurt at some point during the 2024 season and had surgery last March to repair a torn labrum. Injury concerns are likely why Campbell fell to the Eagles with the 31stpick in last year’s draft.

Campbell played in all 18 games last year, starting 10 before Dean returned from rehabbing his knee injury.

But he did appear on the injury report late in the season with what was called a “back/shoulder” injury.

Campbell showed up on the injury list as limited the week before the regular-season finale against Washington, but he was a full participant at practice by Friday and played 62 snaps in the 24-17 loss at the Linc.

But it sounds like he’ll miss all the offseason workouts, including spring OTAs. Considering his lack of experience, that’s important work for any young player. Fangio did seem to think Campbell would be ready to go for training camp in late July.

With Dean gone and Campbell sidelined, the Eagles have Jeremiah Trotter Jr. and Smael Mondon as linebacker options until Campbell returns.

Fangio also spoke about the free agency losses of Dean, Reed Blankenship and Jaelan Phillips from last year’s defense.

“Yeah, it's like we're the farm system defensively for the 31 other teams in the league,” he said. “But we'll do a good job. Howie does a great job putting together the roster. The assistant coaches will do a great job getting the new guys ready, and we'll be ready.

“But we'll miss them. You know, Nikobe had a great two years for us. You know, We're going to miss him. I really enjoyed the guy.

“We lost Reid. Reid was a great story, coming from an undrafted free agent to being a starter, winning a Super Bowl, being an elected captain. You know, he's got a lot of intangibles besides his good play that we'll miss.

“And Jaelen Phillips, he’s a hell of a player, as you saw Carolina thought so, too, with the amount of money they paid him. We were trying hard to keep him, but it got a bit too expensive. And I wish him nothing but the best of luck.”

The one addition on defense is outside forner Riq Woolen, a Pro Bowler in 2022. Woolen’s 12 interceptions since 2022 are 10th-most in the NFL and 4th-most among cornerbacks. He joins Quinyon Mitchell and Cooper DeJean in a loaded cornerback room.

“I haven’t met him yet, I only know him from video study and evaluating him,” Fangio said. “And we're hopeful that he's going to come in here and play very well. If Tariq can turn out to be what we hope he can be, it will be three good cover guys.”

Fangio goes back to the early 1980s with Vermeil. They first crossed paths when Fangio was coaching at his alma mater, Dunmore High outside Scranton.

“When I first started coaching in high school, I went to a clinic where Dick spoke at,” he said. “It was down in Atlantic City, and he was the coach of the Eagles at the time. So I was fired up to go see him, and I even went and got his autograph after he spoke. And I told him that story, and now this many years later, we're good friends. We text before and after every game, text in the offseason, been to each other's house.

“And, you know, to go from that clinic where I was a high school coach, and he was the head coach of the Eagles to now, what a great relationship I have with him. You know, it means a lot.

title: Green Bay Packers Re-sign Key Tight End Depth Piece In 2026 NFL Free Agency Amid ...
description:
The Green Bay Packers may not be massive players in NFL free agency this year.
One of the players that Green Bay wants to extend is tight end Tucker Kraft.
The Green Bay Packers re-sign Josh Whyle amid Tucker Kraft uncertainty Kraft was well on his way to having the best season ever for a Packers tight end when he went down last season.
Without him and Kraft, Luke Musgrave was the only real piece of tight end depth that the Packers had.
In very limited playing time last season, Whyle caught five passes for 36 yards and a touchdown.
text:
The Green Bay Packers may not be massive players in NFL free agency this year. After two seasons of making significant additions (with mixed results) on the free agent market, the prevailing belief is that the Packers are going to invest most of their salary cap resources into contract extensions for several of their own players.

That is not to say that they will not sign any free agents. In fact, they already agreed to a two-year contract with cornerback Benjamin St-Juste. However, fans should not expect them to target any of the bigger names on the market.

One of the players that Green Bay wants to extend is tight end Tucker Kraft. However, they may be waiting to see how he looks when he comes back from his torn ACL.

The Green Bay Packers re-sign Josh Whyle amid Tucker Kraft uncertainty

Kraft was well on his way to having the best season ever for a Packers tight end when he went down last season. In just eight games, he had 32 receptions for 489 receiving yards and six touchdowns. He still finished tied for the team lead in touchdown receptions.

The hope, of course, is that he is able to come back better than ever after his torn ACL. That being said, the team is not sure if he will be able to be ready for the start of the season.

As an insurance policy in the event that he can’t, they have re-signed tight end Josh Whyle, according to Bill Huber of Sports Illustrated:

“It’s a one-year deal for Whyle, who was scheduled to be a restricted free agent. He will provide critical depth at a position in which Tucker Kraft is coming off a torn ACL and John FitzPatrick is a free agent and coming off a torn Achilles.”

As Huber notes, John Fitzpatrick, also a tight end, is not going to play at all in 2026 after suffering a torn Achilles. Without him and Kraft, Luke Musgrave was the only real piece of tight end depth that the Packers had.

With Whyle on the roster again, they will hopefully be in a good position, especially if/when Kraft comes back.

In very limited playing time last season, Whyle caught five passes for 36 yards and a touchdown. He was originally claimed by the Packers off of waivers from the Tennessee Titans. They signed him to the active roster when Kraft got hurt.

title: Source: Ex-Saints TE Foster Moreau to sign with Texans - ESPN
authors: Kevin Seifert, Seth Wickersham, Jordan Raanan, Rich Cimini, Nick Wagoner, Nate Taylor, Marcel Louis-Jacques, Katherine Terrell, Doug Greenberg, Jordan Reid

text:
The Houston Texans have agreed to a contract with free agent tight end Foster Moreau, according to NFL Network.

The move adds depth to the Texans' tight end room, which features Dalton Schultz as the starter.

Moreau, 28, played in 11 games for the New Orleans Saints in 2025 after starting the season on the physically unable to perform list. Moreau was coming off a knee injury that occurred in the 2024 season finale. He caught seven passes for 59 yards last season and missed the final two games of the season due to an ankle injury that was not considered a long-term issue.

The Saints added veteran tight end Noah Fant in the free agent negotiating period.

Moreau has spent the last three seasons in New Orleans after signing with them as a free agent in 2023. He spent the first four seasons of his career with the Raiders, who selected him out of LSU in the fourth round of the 2019 draft. He tied a career-high of five receiving touchdowns in 2024 prior to his injury.

He briefly stepped away from football in the spring of 2023 after he was diagnosed with Hodgkin lymphoma following a physical on a visit with the Saints. He announced that his cancer was in full remission in July 2023.

He has played in 104 games and has caught 151 passes for 1,722 yards and 18 touchdowns in seven seasons.

title: Ten takeaways from Day 1 of NFL free agency, including new-look Chiefs, uneventful Cowboys
description:
The Athletic has live coverage of the 2026 NFL free agency.
Inside: 10 things you should know from the first day of free agency, plus the big names still available.
Free agency: What we learned on day one Over $2 billion in free-agency contracts were agreed to yesterday, so I understand if your head is spinning.
Since 2024, Kansas City running backs have ranked 31st in yards per carry and 32nd in yards after contact per run.
Extra Points 🎙 Live breakdowns of free agency all week on “The Athletic Football Show,” starting at 3 p.m.
text:
The Athletic has live coverage of the 2026 NFL free agency.

Inside: 10 things you should know from the first day of free agency, plus the big names still available. Let’s go.

This article is from Scoop City, The Athletic’s NFL newsletter. Sign up here to receive it directly in your inbox.

Free agency: What we learned on day one

Over $2 billion in free-agency contracts were agreed to yesterday, so I understand if your head is spinning. Before we get into the 10 moves that foreshadow what’s to come in 2026, here’s how The Athletic graded the highest-paid players:

Onto the list:

1. Same old Cowboys? Jerry Jones promised Dallas would spend more than usual in this free-agency period. Naturally, not much of that has happened. The Cowboys did add two serviceable starters, but much more is needed to fix one of the worst defenses in franchise history.

Having recently freed $66 million in cap space, they could still make a splash. The biggest name remaining? Only the No. 1 overall free agent, edge rusher Trey Hendrickson, reportedly seeking over $30 million. 👀

2. Should we let the Dolphins fast forward to 2027? They landed quarterback Malik Willis with a reasonable deal averaging $22.5 million for three years, slightly above what the Jets gave Justin Fields last offseason. Two years guaranteed means it’s essentially a short-term trial for the promising 26-year-old, who joins De’Von Achane and Jaylen Waddle in what should again be the league’s fastest offense.

They might want to sprint past this season. After cutting Tua Tagovailoa, who signed with the Falcons, over half of Miami’s 2026 salary cap is going to players not on the roster. $151 million-plus in dead money!!

3. Expect Jeremiyah Love to be drafted between No. 4 and 7. The Notre Dame running back is a Jahmyr Gibbs-level prospect in a draft devoid of true difference-makers. Love’s landing spots seem to have narrowed to the Titans at No. 4 or Commanders at No. 7, after the holders of picks Nos. 3, 8 and 9 added running backs:

The Cardinals (No. 3) added former Falcons backup Tyler Allgeier and retained James Conner.

The Saints (No. 8) agreed to terms with Travis Etienne yesterday, which could spell the end for Alvin Kamara’s time in New Orleans.

The Chiefs (No. 9) evidently didn’t think Love would fall to them, which brings me to …

4. Kansas City’s new-look offense. Signing the Super Bowl MVP was a wise decision for the Chiefs, whose run game struggled across the past two seasons. Since 2024, Kansas City running backs have ranked 31st in yards per carry and 32nd in yards after contact per run.

“If we can be more explosive and more effective in the running game, we can take a lot of pressure off (Patrick Mahomes), especially coming back early in the season with the injury,” Kansas City general manager Brett Veach said in February.

Enter Walker, an explosive athlete who had more 20-plus-yard runs than every Chiefs running back combined last season (12 to one if we add playoffs), including this in the Super Bowl:

The 25-year-old finished fifth in explosive run rate last season and was the highest-ranked free-agent running back available. A perfect upgrade, I’d say. He’ll work under the returning Eric Bieniemy to help lead a newly physical Chiefs offense.

5. Mike Evans’ new digs. The 49ers brought in the 12-year veteran with a three-year, $20 million-per offer, one filled with incentives. How does he respond after his first truly injury-plagued season?

A dominant, six-catch, 132-yard Week 15 suggests there’s still something in the tank as he enters his age-33 season. The 6-foot-5 receiver is a strong fit for Kyle Shanahan’s offense as a man-coverage beater, offering Brock Purdy a towering red zone target and deep threat.

6. You won’t recognize the Raiders. On Sunday night, Las Vegas had one player making over $10 million. They added seven yesterday, and general manager John Spytek and new coach Klint Kubiak made (mostly) savvy moves, as graded by my colleagues Mike Jones and Daniel Popper.

Most notable was center Tyler Linderbaum, whose $27 million average was a staggering 50 percent jump above the next highest-paid center, Kansas City’s Creed Humphrey ($18M). Unheard of, but don’t call it an overpay for the three-time Pro Bowler. Linderbaum offers veteran leadership, protecting an expected rookie quarterback and clearing lanes for Ashton Jeanty.

7. The Panthers are here to stay. After their first playoff appearance in seven years, Carolina just added two of the top three overall free agents: Eagles edge Jaelan Phillips and Jaguars linebacker Devin Lloyd. Those additions weren’t cheap — $20 million per year for Phillips and $15 million for Lloyd — but add enormous value to a suddenly ascending defense that has the makings of a top-10 unit.

8. The Rams are self-aware. Seattle exposed their weakness in the NFC title game, when Sam Darnold threw for 346 yards and three touchdowns against an overmatched secondary.

That didn’t sit well with general manager Les Snead. He’s since traded a first-round pick and change to make Trent McDuffie the league’s highest-paid corner, then paired the former Chief with a Kansas City teammate, corner Jaylen Watson. Add the return of nickel Quentin Lake, and this cornerback room is suddenly a strength. Well done.

9. The Jets defense improved. Last year, we ranked all 32 teams by how successful they were at signing free agents. Guess who finished last?

Hint: That same team just addressed significant needs via free agency, adding leadership with the seemingly ageless, 37-year-old linebacker Demario Davis ($11 million per year) and pass rush depth with two mid-level edge rushers. Also adding veteran safety Minkah Fitzpatrick and a potential pass rusher at No. 2, this historically bad defense could make a significant jump.

10. Even Cincinnati’s defense got better. The Bengals are adding promising edge Boye Mafe (three years, $20 million per) and safety Bryan Cook (three years, $13.5 million per), quality defenders who should improve a unit that had the league’s most missed tackles last season.

As our Paul Dehner Jr. explains, “These were legitimate top-of-the-market spends at two critical positions. Depending on what happens at linebacker, they could add a top-25 overall free agent to each level of the defense.”

Lastly, keep an eye on undervalued signings. The Buccaneers added Lions linebacker Alex Anzalone, while the Chargers landed run-blocking tight end Charlie Kolar from Baltimore. They were both A+ signings, per those grades by Jones and Popper.

What does it mean for your fantasy team? We broke down each offseason move here. Kenneth Walker stock 📈 📈.

Who’s still available?

While Cincinnati’s 31-year-old star pass rusher remains available, Hendrickson isn’t the only big name still negotiating. Each of these prominent players could still be strong additions (age as of 2026’s Week 1):

QB Kyler Murray (29). His salary will be irrelevant here, since he is due $36.8 million from the Cardinals and his next contract will just reduce what they owe him. The Browns, Jets and Vikings are potential landing spots for 2019’s No. 1 pick.

WR Tyreek Hill (32). He was still an elite receiver prior to last season’s knee injury, ranking No. 4 in yards per route run. Expect an incentive-laden deal, reducing his next team’s risk. Kansas City return?

QB Aaron Rodgers (42). The Steelers’ swing for receiver Michael Pittman should help entice Rodgers to return to Pittsburgh, as they’ve finally added a legitimate No. 2 across from DK Metcalf.

While those are the best-known players, none ranks among the 10 best still available. For those, read our full list of the top remaining free agents.

Extra Points

🎙 Live breakdowns of free agency all week on “The Athletic Football Show,” starting at 3 p.m. ET. It’ll be on in my house all week. You can listen here.

📋 Draft order. The NFL released 33 compensatory picks for the 2026 draft, with the Eagles, Steelers and 49ers each landing multiple top-140 picks.

📃 Patriots get risky. New England agreed to a deal with the often-injured, high-potential guard Alijah Vera-Tucker. The 26-year-old missed the Jets’ entire 2025 season with a torn bicep and has averaged eight games played per season, but is a strong option when healthy.

📊 Seahawks encore? It helps that receiver/returner Rashid Shaheed is coming back, though Seattle has lost plenty of talent. Michael-Shawn Dugar recaps the Seahawks’ first day here.

▶️ Yesterday’s most-clicked: Let’s not forget Rondale Moore so quickly.

📫 Enjoyed this read? Sign up here to receive The Athletic’s free NFL newsletter in your inbox.

title: Giants' Cam Skattebo Speaks Out on Injury Recovery & Timeline - Heavy Sports
description:
Fortunately for the Giants, Skattebo is making significant progress in his injury rehab, as he revealed on The Rich Eisen Show this week.
Connor Hughes of SNY believes wide receiver Brian Thomas Jr. could be a trade target for the Giants.
“I could see the Giants potentially getting available because they made calls on him as well is Brian Thomas of the Jaguars is available,” Hughes said.
“He is somebody the Jaguars are entertaining offers to.” Former Giants star running back Tiki Barber thinks Thomas would be an “interesting” addition alongside Nabers.
Brian Thomas is a really good wide receiver.
text:
The New York Giants have undergone drastic changes after finishing the 2025 NFL season with a disappointing 4-13 record, securing the No. 5 overall pick in the 2026 NFL Draft.

Along with bringing in one-time Super Bowl champion head coach John Harbaugh to replace Brian Daboll, the Giants have a bright future ahead, with a rising star quarterback under center in Jaxson Dart, one of the most talented young wide receivers downfield in Malik Nabers and an up-and-coming running back in Cam Skattebo.

Giants Star Cam Skattebo Gives an Injury Update

Even though the future is bright in the Big Apple, with one of the best head coaches in the NFL leading the way, two of the team’s young stars are working their way back from season-ending injuries, as Nabers suffered a torn ACL and Skattebo went down with a gruesome ankle injury.

Fortunately for the Giants, Skattebo is making significant progress in his injury rehab, as he revealed on The Rich Eisen Show this week.

“I’m getting better,” Skattebo said. “I ran for the first time today about 75%, so it’s the bright side of things.”

Eisen asked Skattebo how fast he could run at 75 percent, and Skattebo gave a hilarious, classic response that only he would give.

“Not very fast, I’ll tell you that. … I can get away from like a baby hippo maybe, but that’s about it. A little bit over a jog. Kind of just striding out, not exploding yet and getting into that part of the game, but the jog and the striding.”

After giving an update on his injury rehab, Skattebo let it be known that he will be ready to take the field once the Giants’ training camp gets underway.

“I’ll be ready to go by training camp,” Skattebo said. “I probably got a little over a month left till I’m fully back to 100%. So OTAs are coming up in about a month and I’m excited. I’ll be ready to go.”

Although he’s still got some rehabbing to do, he’s clearly headed in the right direction, which is great news for the Giants.

Giants Legend Tiki Barber Chimes in on Brian Thomas Jr.

With Wan’Dale Robinson agreeing to sign with the Tennessee Titans, reuniting him with Daboll, the Giants’ brass will likely be looking for a viable wide receiver to bring in to join Nabers and Darius Slayton.

Connor Hughes of SNY believes wide receiver Brian Thomas Jr. could be a trade target for the Giants.

“I could see the Giants potentially getting available because they made calls on him as well is Brian Thomas of the Jaguars is available,” Hughes said. “He is somebody the Jaguars are entertaining offers to.”

Former Giants star running back Tiki Barber thinks Thomas would be an “interesting” addition alongside Nabers.

“That sounds interesting to me,” Barber said of the Giants’ potential pursuit of Thomas. “You bring Malik Nabers back with his college teammate. Brian Thomas is a really good wide receiver. He’s had injury issues, so that’s the one thing you worry about.”

Only time will tell what the Giants’ brass choose to do, but they’ll have some options to explore, including trading for Thomas.

The Warriors will continue to play without their best player.

Steph Curry, who has missed the last 15 games with a knee injury, will be re-evaluated in another 10 days, the team announced Wednesday. ESPN's Shams Charania and Anthony Slater were first to report the news, adding that

The star point guard has begun on-court work and is aiming to intensify his workouts.

That puts Curry out for at least another five games, with matchups against the Minnesota Timberwolves, New York Knicks, Washington Wizards, Boston Celtics and Detroit Pistons.

At that point, Golden State has 12 regular-season games remaining, and depending on how the team holds up without its star player, it could determine whether Curry returns this season or if the Warriors just shut him down for the remainder of the wobbly campaign.

Warriors general manager Mike Dunleavy shared last week that sidelining Curry for the rest of the season is unlikely, but acknowledged it can't be ruled out.

As of the time of this writing, the Warriors are 32-33 and the ninth seed in the Western Conference -- a half-game behind the Los Angeles Clippers and 1.5 games ahead of the 10th-place Portland Trail Blazers.

text:
The Seattle Seahawks have re-signed safety AJ Finley, according to Finley’s agency, SportsTrust Advisors.

Seattle Seahawks Offseason Tracker: Free agents, draft, more

Finley, a 24-year-old restricted free agent, was a candidate to earn a backup safety role for the Seahawks this past season, but landed on injured reserve on Aug. 4 with a knee injury suffered in practice and missed the rest of the season.

Finley, who went undrafted out of Ole Miss in 2023, was claimed off waivers by the Seahawks on Nov. 26, 2024. He appeared in four of Seattle’s final six games that season, recording one tackle while playing six snaps on defense and 62 on special teams.

A Mobile, Ala., native, Finley originally signed with the Los Angeles Chargers in 2023. He had three tackles and a forced fumble over 12 games during his rookie season, which included playing 200 snaps on special teams.

Finley appeared in 10 games for Los Angeles in 2024 before he was released on Nov. 25. He had 11 tackles while continuing to get the majority of his snaps on special teams.

Finley was a second-team All-SEC selection as junior at Ole Miss in 2021.

title: Maxx Crosby's wife reacts to Ravens trade fallout with three-word message - Fox News
description:
On Tuesday, the Ravens pulled out of a trade that would have brought the star pass rusher to Baltimore for multiple draft picks.
"The Baltimore Ravens have backed out of our trade agreement for Maxx Crosby," the Raiders said in a statement.
The sudden reversal drew a sharp response from Crosby’s wife, Rachel, who defended her husband and pushed back on claims about his knee recovery.
Despite learning his knee would require offseason surgery, Crosby continued to play through the injury, according to reports.
He also acknowledged the challenges the Ravens' medical staff face in assessing future risk, particularly given Crosby’s stage in the recovery process.
text:
NEWYou can now listen to Fox News articles!

Maxx Crosby's farewell to the Las Vegas Raiders may have been premature. On Tuesday, the Ravens pulled out of a trade that would have brought the star pass rusher to Baltimore for multiple draft picks.

"The Baltimore Ravens have backed out of our trade agreement for Maxx Crosby," the Raiders said in a statement. "We will have no further comment at this time." ESPN, citing league sources, reported that the Ravens decision centered around medical concerns that surfaced during Crosby's physical. NFL trades hinge on players clearing a physical.

The sudden reversal drew a sharp response from Crosby’s wife, Rachel, who defended her husband and pushed back on claims about his knee recovery.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

In an Instagram Stories post, Rachel shared a tweet from Crosby’s agent stating the defensive end remains "on track" in his recovery from a knee injury.

"Maxx continues to be on track in his recovery and if anything is ahead of schedule according to his surgeon Dr. Neal ElAttrache," C.J. LaBoy wrote. "Maxx remains on track to return during the offseason program & will undoubtedly return as the dominant game wrecker he has been these past 7 seasons."

JETS TO REUNITE WITH GENO SMITH AFTER RAIDERS GAIN DRAFT PICK IN TRADE: REPORTS

Rachel echoed LaBoy’s comments, adding "Say it louder," along with a pair of clapping emojis. The NFL star later shared the tweet on his social media platform.

Crosby had surgery in January to address the torn meniscus in his left knee. Despite learning his knee would require offseason surgery, Crosby continued to play through the injury, according to reports. The Raiders shut down Crosby during the final stretch of the 2025 season, a move that appeared to frustrate the five-time Pro Bowler.

Dr. Neal ElAttrache, Crosby’s surgeon, offered an update last week on the 28-year-old’s recovery.

"Maxx Crosby is doing very well in the early part of his rehab and recovery from surgery to treat a significant meniscus tear and the related stress injury to the bone and cartilage in his knee. He is certainly on track in his planned program….he feels very well and is already much improved compared to before surgery," ElAttrache told ESPN.

He also acknowledged the challenges the Ravens' medical staff face in assessing future risk, particularly given Crosby’s stage in the recovery process.

"We truly respect the work of the Ravens staff with Maxx regarding the contract physical exam. We also understand the challenge the staff faces when tasked to provide a future risk assessment based on an evaluation early in the recovery process. This is especially challenging when dealing with an elite player like Maxx considering the level of commitment necessary for a team to obtain him. The timing of this assessment is unfortunate because the apparent risk will lessen as his recovery progresses and his return to performance over the next few months becomes clear."

CLICK HERE TO GET THE FOX NEWS APP

Crosby and the Raiders reached an agreement on the three-year extension last March.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

text:
Now that Daniel Jones and the Indianapolis Colts have agreed to a new deal, when could he be back on the football field?

Jones' 2025 season with the Colts was cut short due to an Achilles injury he suffered in Week 14. According to ESPN's Adam Schefter, Jones is "expected to be ready for the start of the regular season."

Another source would tell Schefter that he even "expects him (Jones) to be ready for training camp."

Jones offered a similar timeline back in January, mentioning at that time that he could be back on the field for the start of training camp.

Despite an Achilles injury being a major one to come back from, as evidenced by Jones' new deal, GM Chris Ballard has said that he is "confident" in Jones' ability to recover and bounce back.

Jones and the Colts' offense were off to red-hot starts last season. The Colts believe that the success that Jones and the team had can be replicated and sustained in 2026.

title: Giants RB Cam Skattebo Likens Injury Recovery to Outrunning a 'Baby Hippo' - Newsweek
description:
New York Giants fans quickly attached to the team's rookie duo, quarterback Jaxson Dart and running back Cam Skattebo, last season.
Dart and Skattebo frequently went viral for their headbutt celebration, and their bruising style of play captured league-wide attention.
The dawn of the Dawn and Skattebo era in New York hit its apex when the Giants upset the then-defending champion Philadelphia Eagles 34-17 on Thursday, Oct. 9.
Skattebo went off for 98 yards and three touchdowns, while Dart accounted for 253 yards and two touchdowns — 195 yards and one touchdown through the air, 58 yards and one touchdown on the ground.
Skattebo finished his rookie campaign with 410 yards and five touchdowns on 101 carries and 4.1 yards per attempt in eight games.
text:
New York Giants fans quickly attached to the team's rookie duo, quarterback Jaxson Dart and running back Cam Skattebo, last season. Dart and Skattebo frequently went viral for their headbutt celebration, and their bruising style of play captured league-wide attention.

The dawn of the Dawn and Skattebo era in New York hit its apex when the Giants upset the then-defending champion Philadelphia Eagles 34-17 on Thursday, Oct. 9. Skattebo went off for 98 yards and three touchdowns, while Dart accounted for 253 yards and two touchdowns — 195 yards and one touchdown through the air, 58 yards and one touchdown on the ground.

Unfortunately, the Giants' momentum was derailed when they met the Eagles again on Oct. 26. Skattebo suffered a heinous season-ending injury. According to ESPN's Jordan Raanan, he dislocated his ankle, suffered an open tibia fracture, and ruptured his deltoid ligament.

On Wednesday, Skattebo joined The Rich Eisen Show and provided an update on his rehabilitation.

"I'm getting better," the 24-year-old said. "I ran for the first time today at about 75%, so it's the bright side of things."

When Eisen asked what 75% looked like, Skattebo said, "Not very fast, I'll tell you that. I can get away from, like, a baby hippo, maybe. That's about it. A little bit over a job. Kind of just striding out, not exploding yet, and getting into that part of the game, but the jog and the striding."

Skattebo said the only thing slowing him down is the nature of his injury, but he'll "absolutely" be ready for training camp in July. "I've probably got a little over a month left until I'm fully back to 100%," he said. "OTAs are coming up in about a month, and I'm excited. I'll be ready to go."

Skattebo also provided details from his initial conversations with newly hired Giants head coach John Harbaugh, who coached the Baltimore Ravens from 2008 until last season. "He told me he respects the way I run the ball and the offense that he's implementing is downhill running, and it's perfect for what I am," he said.

Skattebo finished his rookie campaign with 410 yards and five touchdowns on 101 carries and 4.1 yards per attempt in eight games.

Skattebo's comments echo what SNY's Connor Hughes reported earlier this month after the 2026 NFL Combine.

"Harbaugh has a clear vision for the team’s offensive identity: run the ball — and then run it again — behind a dominant offensive line," Hughes wrote. "That makes the addition of Greg Roman to the coaching staff particularly notable."

text:
In their first order of business to kick off the new league year, the Detroit Lions released defensive end Josh Paschal.

Paschal spent the entire 2025 season on the nonfootball injury last after undergoing back surgery. His contract tolled for 2026 and the Lions could have kept him on their roster, but general manager Brad Holmes was noncommittal about Paschal's future in late February at the NFL combine.

"Josh, we’ll have the ability to bring him back," Holmes said. "There's still discussions to be had about that one, but yeah, it's a possibility."

A second-round pick at No. 46 overall out of Kentucky in 2022, Paschal had five sacks while playing as a rotational defensive end and part-time starter in his first three NFL seasons, when he missed 15 games with an assortment of ailments.

The Lions remain in the market for defensive line help after failing to land a pass rusher during the free agent negotiating period.

Aidan Hutchinson and Ahmed Hassanein are the top defensive ends on the roster, though Levi Onwuzurike – whose contract also tolled after he missed the year following knee surgery – also can play the position. Hassanein, a sixth-round pick last year, did not play in 2025.

Al-Quadin Muhammad, who finished second on the Lions with a career-high 11 sacks, and Marcus Davenport are free agents.

The Lions have added four players in free agency so far, agreeing to deals with offensive linemen Cade Mays and Larry Borom, quarterback Teddy Bridgewater and running back Isiah Pacheco, and agreed to new deals with cornerback Rock Ya-Sin and linebacker Malcolm Rodriguez.

They've lost four contributors in Alex Anzalone, Amik Robertson, Roy Lopez and Kalif Raymond, plus backup quarterback Kyle Allen.

title: Warriors star Steph Curry to miss more time due to knee injury, to be re-evaluated in 10 days
description:
SAN FRANCISCO — Golden State Warriors star Stephen Curry will miss at least six more games as he continues to deal with a lingering runner’s knee injury and will be re-evaluated in 10 days, the team said Wednesday.
Those workouts will continue to “intensify” as long as Curry’s knee continues to feel better while he ramps up his rehab.
He has missed the last 15 games and is now on track to miss at least six more.
The good news for the Warriors is that six of their last 17 games are against tanking and/or bad teams.
The return of Curry, who is averaging 27.2 points this season, can’t come soon enough for the Warriors.
text:
SAN FRANCISCO — Golden State Warriors star Stephen Curry will miss at least six more games as he continues to deal with a lingering runner’s knee injury and will be re-evaluated in 10 days, the team said Wednesday.

The Warriors said that Curry, who turns 38 on Saturday, is making “good progress” in his return and has started to do some individual on-court work. Those workouts will continue to “intensify” as long as Curry’s knee continues to feel better while he ramps up his rehab.

Curry has not played since leaving a loss to the Detroit Pistons early on Jan. 30. He has missed the last 15 games and is now on track to miss at least six more.

The hope within the organization was that the two-time MVP would be able to return after the All-Star break, but that didn’t happen as he continued to deal with pain in his knee. The Warriors have gone 5-10 in Curry’s absence and have slid to ninth in the Western Conference standings, 1 1/2 games in front of the Portland Trail Blazers. Their next game is Friday at home against the Minnesota Timberwolves, after which they embark on a six-game East Coast road trip beginning Sunday night at the New York Knicks.

The reality for the Warriors (32-33) is that even if Curry can return soon, their place among Play-In teams is unlikely to change in either direction. They are now six games behind the seventh-place Phoenix Suns with 17 games left to play. It’s a place that Warriors coach Steve Kerr and his team have been resigned to for the last couple of weeks as they have held out hope that Curry can return.

The Warriors know that they always have a chance to win when Curry is on the floor, and are not yet seriously considering the possibility that he could be ruled out for the season. Curry still wants to play — as evidenced by the work he is putting in behind the scenes — and the team would at least like to get a look at how he looks playing alongside new acquisition Kristaps Porzingis.

Beyond that, as the standings illustrate, only tanking and/or bad teams remain behind the Blazers in the standings. There’s only so far the Warriors could drop, even if they wanted to. The Memphis Grizzlies, currently in 11th place in the West, are 23-41 and have already made deals to ensure they can continue losing down the stretch.

Ideally, the Warriors’ front office would like Curry to come back, see if they can win a few postseason games, and provide a little momentum heading into the offseason. But given that the Warriors just lost back-to-back games to the tanking Utah Jazz and Chicago Bulls, it is clear that they have to play almost perfectly without Curry to beat any team.

The good news for the Warriors is that six of their last 17 games are against tanking and/or bad teams. In the short term, as Curry continues to be out, look for Kerr to continue to rely on a trifecta of guards in Brandin Podziemski, Pat Spencer and LJ Cryer to help set up the offense. As Kerr has shown in recent games, he will go to whichever one of the three is playing well at the moment.

The return of Curry, who is averaging 27.2 points this season, can’t come soon enough for the Warriors. But even the magic he provides on the floor won’t be enough to lift them out of the standings predicament they find themselves in.
